subtopics.summary: Students conduct simple experiments and observations related to water movement and moon phases.

subtopics.name: Information Skills, Projects
subtopics.summary: Students practice information gathering, discussion, and observation skills through projects on sunrise/sunset, temperature, and sky observation.

subtopics.name: Communication through Mapping Skills, Drawing Pictures and Making Models
subtopics.summary: Students develop artistic and model-making skills by creating representations of celestial phenomena and the solar system.

subtopics.name: Appreciation, Values and Creating Awareness towards Bio-diversity
subtopics.summary: Students reflect on the importance of the atmosphere, the sun, and cultural practices related to celestial bodies, and express their observations.

subtopics.name: Self-assessment Checklist
subtopics.summary: Students evaluate their ability to explain concepts, draw diagrams, analyze information, and create models related to the chapter's topics.

subtopics.name: Think and say...
subtopics.summary: Students answer questions based on a story about a family taking safety precautions before a trip.

subtopics.name: Group work
subtopics.summary: Students discuss potential accidents and safety measures at home, school, on the road, and when children are alone.

subtopics.name: Observe the pictures and discuss
subtopics.summary: Students observe pictures depicting various accident scenarios such as an open borewell, open manhole, talking on a cellphone while driving, overcrowded vehicles, fire, and road accidents.

subtopics.name: Group work
subtopics.summary: Students discuss the types of accidents that can occur based on the observed pictures and the safety measures to prevent them, specifically focusing on fire and road accidents.

subtopics.name: Group work
subtopics.summary: Students discuss types of accidents in crowded places, necessary precautionary measures, and reasons for fire accidents in villages during summer.

subtopics.name: Safety measures in Jataras
subtopics.summary: This subtopic lists specific safety measures to be taken in crowded places like Jataras, such as ensuring safe drinking water, proper parking, police assistance, fire engines, barricades, and public announcements.

subtopics.name: Think and say...
subtopics.summary: Students answer questions about the causes, locations, and prevention measures for water accidents.

subtopics.name: Observe the picture and discuss
subtopics.summary: Students observe pictures of earthquake damage and discuss the causes and consequences.

subtopics.name: Things to be done during earth quakes.
subtopics.summary: This subtopic lists specific safety actions to take during an earthquake, such as seeking shelter under sturdy objects, staying away from breakable items, avoiding lifts, moving to open areas, driving slowly, helping the injured, and disconnecting power.

subtopics.name: Observe the picture, think and say.
subtopics.summary: Students observe pictures of flooded areas and discuss what they see, when floods happen, the problems people face, and precautionary measures.

subtopics.name: First Aid
subtopics.summary: Students learn the definition and importance of first aid, emphasizing that it is immediate help given to a patient or victim before medical care arrives, to reduce fear and instill confidence.

subtopics.name: First aid box
subtopics.summary: Students learn about the necessity of having a first aid box in schools and buses, and the importance of applying antiseptic lotions to wounds.

subtopics.name: Life saving principles
subtopics.summary: Students are introduced to three life-saving principles in first aid: making the patient lie on their back, checking for breathing and providing artificial respiration, and observing the heart beat and performing chest compressions if there is no response.

subtopics.name: In case of sprains
subtopics.summary: Students learn how to provide first aid for sprains, including not rubbing the ointment, applying ice, and bandaging the sprained part.

subtopics.name: In case of burns
subtopics.summary: Students learn first aid for burns, including applying cold water, not pinching blisters, not rubbing, not applying ice, and following the SDR (Stop-Drop-Roll) principle for fire accidents.

subtopics.name: In case of consumption of poison
subtopics.summary: Students learn that in case of poison consumption, water should be given to decrease the intensity, and the person should not vomit if unconscious.

subtopics.name: In case of unconsciousness
subtopics.summary: Students learn that an unconscious person should be made to lie on one side, keeping the chin up to prevent the tongue from blocking respiration.

subtopics.name: In case of heart attack
subtopics.summary: Students learn to recognize symptoms of a heart attack (pinpricking sensation, intense sweating, nausea, chest pain) and to seek medical help immediately if no medicines are available.

subtopics.name: In case of a fracture
subtopics.summary: Students learn that a fractured part should not be moved while taking the patient to the hospital.

subtopics.name: How to recognise paralysis?
subtopics.summary: Students learn how to recognize paralysis by observing symptoms like nausea, senselessness, difficulty laughing, and an odd or crooked mouth shape, and the importance of taking the person to the hospital within the golden hour.

subtopics.name: In case of dog bite
subtopics.summary: Students learn that a bitten area (by monkey, dog, cat, etc.) should be washed with soap, but the wound should not be covered with cloth or bandage.

subtopics.name: In case of snake bite
subtopics.summary: Students learn that snake bites are poisonous and can cause fear, and that steps should be taken to reduce the person's fear and prevent them from becoming unconscious.

subtopics.name: In case of sun stroke
subtopics.summary: Students learn that sunstroke symptoms include high temperature, nausea, vomiting, and severe headache, and that the person should not be allowed to drink water but should have their body wiped with a cold cloth or be given ORS solution/electral water.

subtopics.name: In case of chemical falling in eyes:
subtopics.summary: Students learn that if chemicals fall into eyes, they should be kept open under cool running water for 15-20 minutes, and the eyes should not be rubbed.

subtopics.name: In case of unconsciousness
subtopics.summary: Students learn detailed steps for handling unconsciousness due to lack of food or exhaustion, including lifting legs and head, and keeping legs in a higher position than the head to improve blood supply to the brain.

subtopics.name: In case of heart failure
subtopics.summary: Students learn about CPR (Cardio Pulmonary Resuscitation) as a first aid measure for heart attack, electric shock, or other reasons, including the steps for chest compressions and artificial respiration.

subtopics.name: In case of a block in the throat
subtopics.summary: Students learn how to help someone with a blocked throat by asking them to bend forward and hitting their back, or asking them to cough.

subtopics.name: How to stop bleeding
subtopics.summary: Students learn how to stop bleeding by pressing the part hard and keeping it in a raised position, and seeking hospital care for heavy bleeding.

subtopics.name: In case of bleeding nose
subtopics.summary: Students learn how to stop a bleeding nose by bending the head forward, pressing the soft part of the nose, and not using cotton to stop the bleeding, especially for those with B.P. issues.

subtopics.name: In case of electric shock
subtopics.summary: Students learn that in case of electric shock, the connection should be switched off, the person should be removed from the plug, and artificial respiration or CPR should be given if necessary.

subtopics.name: Who helps?
subtopics.summary: Students learn about the 108 emergency service, which provides assistance for fire, road accidents, and natural calamities, and how to use it to prevent accidents and illness.

subtopics.name: Drawing emergency vehicles
subtopics.summary: Students practice drawing pictures of emergency vehicles such as a fire engine and a 108 ambulance.

subtopics.name: Appreciation for emergency services
subtopics.summary: Students discuss the importance of appreciating the services provided by emergency staff, such as those associated with the 108 ambulance service.

subtopics.name: Helping during natural calamities
subtopics.summary: Students explore different types of help needed during natural calamities like floods, cyclones, and fire accidents, and discuss how they can assist victims.

subtopics.name: Understanding the need for first aid
subtopics.summary: Students discuss the necessity of first aid and what actions they can take if they learn first aid techniques.

subtopics.name: Critical thinking about traditional remedies
subtopics.summary: Students are presented with a scenario involving a snake bite and a traditional healer (wizard/manthragadu), prompting them to think critically about appropriate responses in such situations.

subtopics.name: Ability to discuss safety measures
subtopics.summary: Students assess their ability to talk about and explain safety measures applicable in various environments.

subtopics.name: Knowledge of emergency vehicles
subtopics.summary: Students assess their ability to ask questions and gather more information about emergency vehicles like 108 and 104.

subtopics.name: Accident observation and recording
subtopics.summary: Students assess their ability to observe and record details pertaining to an accident.

subtopics.name: Collecting emergency contact information
subtopics.summary: Students assess their ability to collect contact details from health workers/hospitals and emergency service phone numbers.

subtopics.name: Helping in natural calamities
subtopics.summary: Students assess their perceived ability to provide assistance during natural calamities.

subtopics.name: Observation of Historical Forts
subtopics.summary: Students identify and recognize various historical forts in Telangana from provided images.

subtopics.name: Discussion on Historical Sites
subtopics.summary: Students discuss their knowledge about the forts, their locations, and their personal experiences or feelings about visiting such places.

subtopics.name: Introduction to Wanaparthy Fort and its Cultural Significance
subtopics.summary: Students learn about Wanaparthy Fort as a historical monument reflecting the traditions and history of Telangana, and understand the importance of preserving such sites.

subtopics.name: Identification of Other Telangana Forts
subtopics.summary: Students are introduced to a list of other significant forts in Telangana State, such as Orugallu, Gadwal, Golconda, Rachakonda, Devarakonda, Bhuvanagiri, Domakonda, and Wanaparti.

subtopics.name: Wanaparthy Fort Location and Name Origin
subtopics.summary: Students learn about the geographical location of Wanaparthy Fort and the historical reason behind its name, relating to 'vanam' (forest).

subtopics.name: Forts and their Builders
subtopics.summary: Students identify various historical monuments, their districts, and the dynasties or individuals responsible for their construction through a provided table.

subtopics.name: History of Wanaparthy Estate and Capital Shifts
subtopics.summary: Students learn about Janum Veera Krishna Reddy, the founder of Wanaparthy estate, the establishment of Sugur Estate, and the historical shifts in the capital of the Wanaparthy estate from Patapalli to Wanaparthy.

subtopics.name: The 'Seven Seas' (Saptha Samudralu) Irrigation System
subtopics.summary: Students learn about the 'Seven Seas' irrigation facilities provided by the kings of Wanaparthy estate and their importance for agriculture and drinking water, along with a list of these seven seas.

subtopics.name: Artistic Designs and Handicrafts
subtopics.summary: Students learn about the artistic designs found on the walls and roofs of buildings constructed by the Reddy Kings, and understand the encouragement given to music, dance, and handicrafts.

subtopics.name: Temple Construction by Wanaparthy Kings
subtopics.summary: Students learn about the temples built by the Wanaparthy Kings, including the Sri Ranganayaka Swamy Temple, and appreciate their architectural and sculptural significance.

subtopics.name: Patronage of Literature by Reddy Kings
subtopics.summary: Students learn about the Reddy Kings' support for literature, recognizing King Bahiri Gopala Rao as a scholar and identifying other notable literary figures associated with the estate.

subtopics.name: Estate Administration and Currency
subtopics.summary: Students learn about how the Wanaparthy estate was administered, its division into regions, and the role of the Nizam's kingdom in its protection. They also learn about 'Sugur Sikkas' as a historical currency.

subtopics.name: Introduction to Energy and its sources for movement
subtopics.summary: Students are introduced to the concept of energy by observing how different vehicles and machines operate and what they require to function, linking it to the energy needed by living beings.

subtopics.name: Energy requirements for gadgets and daily activities
subtopics.summary: Students identify the energy sources needed for common household items and activities, understanding that different forms of energy are used for various purposes.

subtopics.name: 13.3.1 Non - Renewable Resources
subtopics.summary: Students learn about non-renewable energy resources like coal, kerosene, gas, petrol, and diesel, which are obtained from burning fuels and deplete over time as they are fossil fuels formed over millions of years.

subtopics.name: 13.3.2 Renewable Resources
subtopics.summary: Students learn about renewable energy resources such as sunlight, water, and wind, which are not exhausted even after continuous use.

subtopics.name: Uses and generation of solar energy
subtopics.summary: Students explore the various applications of solar energy in daily life and understand the basic principle of how solar cells convert sunlight into electricity.

subtopics.name: Understanding and applications of wind energy
subtopics.summary: Students learn about wind as a source of energy, how it is harnessed using windmills to generate electricity, and its various uses.

subtopics.name: Hydroelectricity generation and types of power
subtopics.summary: Students learn how water energy is converted into electricity through hydropower plants, using the Nagarjuna Sagar Project as an example, and distinguish between hydroelectricity and thermal electricity.

subtopics.name: Comparing renewable and non-renewable energy resources
subtopics.summary: Students compare the characteristics of non-renewable and renewable energy resources, understanding their availability, cost, environmental impact, and the importance of shifting towards sustainable alternatives.

subtopics.name: Importance of energy conservation
subtopics.summary: Students understand the necessity of conserving energy resources to ensure their availability for the future and to reduce environmental impact.

subtopics.name: Practical methods for saving fuels
subtopics.summary: Students learn and apply methods to reduce fuel consumption in transportation and daily activities.

subtopics.name: Practical methods for saving electricity
subtopics.summary: Students learn and apply methods to reduce electricity consumption in homes and other settings.

subtopics.name: General conservation practices
subtopics.summary: Students learn broader conservation practices including avoiding waste of natural resources like water and food, and responsible disposal of materials.

subtopics.name: Conceptual Understanding
subtopics.summary: Students answer questions to demonstrate their understanding of fuels, solar energy appliances, renewable and non-renewable energy resources, electricity saving, and the importance of energy conservation.

subtopics.name: Questioning and Hypotheses
subtopics.summary: Students engage in oral communication by asking parents about electricity saving and inquiring with electricity officers about reasons for power cuts.

subtopics.name: Experiments - Field Observations
subtopics.summary: Students observe and record their household electricity consumption and bill amount over a month.

subtopics.name: Information skills, Projects
subtopics.summary: Students conduct a mini-project by collecting data on electricity consumption and bill amounts from friends' houses and documenting the reasons for consumption.

subtopics.name: Communication through Mapping Skills, Drawing Pictures and Making Models
subtopics.summary: Students demonstrate their understanding of energy production by drawing pictures of hydroelectricity generation, wind mills, and household electric appliances.

subtopics.name: Appreciation, Values and Creating Awareness towards Bio-diversity
subtopics.summary: Students reflect on alternative fuels, compare different energy sources, and consider ways to create awareness about saving water and electricity.

subtopics.name: Self-Assessment Checklist
subtopics.summary: A checklist for students to confirm their understanding and practical skills related to saving fuel and electricity, observing consumption, and explaining energy generation.

subtopics.name: Introduction to India
subtopics.summary: Students learn about India through a song, its identity as a country, its administrative structure (states, union territories, capital), geographical features like mountains, and its rich historical and cultural background.

subtopics.name: 14.2. India and its regions
subtopics.summary: Students learn about the six major geographical regions of India: Eastern, Western, Northern, Southern, North-Eastern, and Central India, along with the states that belong to each region.

subtopics.name: Continents
subtopics.summary: Students learn about the seven continents of the world, their names, and observe them on a world map. They also learn about different landforms like mountains, plateaus, and plains, with examples.

subtopics.name: 14.4. Water region
subtopics.summary: Students learn about oceans and seas, their names, and characteristics. The concept of islands and peninsulas is introduced. The water cycle is explained, and the composition and features of ocean water are discussed.

subtopics.name: Introduction to the Constitution
subtopics.summary: Students learn about the basic idea of a constitution as a set of rules and regulations for a country, and the role of leaders like the President and Prime Minister.

subtopics.name: Formation of the Indian Constitution
subtopics.summary: Students learn about India's independence, the formation of the Drafting Committee, and the role of Dr. Bheem Rao Baba Saheb Ambedkar in framing the Constitution.

subtopics.name: Understanding the Preamble
subtopics.summary: Students learn that the Preamble is a foreword to the constitution, explaining its objectives and serving as the heart of the constitution.

subtopics.name: Key Terms in the Preamble
subtopics.summary: Students are introduced to key terms like Sovereign, Socialist, Secular, Democratic, Republic, Justice, Liberty, Equality, and Fraternity as defined in the Preamble.

subtopics.name: 15.3.1. We the people of India means...
subtopics.summary: Students learn that "We the people of India" refers to all citizens of the country, understanding India's diversity in population and languages.

subtopics.name: 15.3.2. Sovereign, Socialist, Secular, Democratic, Republic state means...
subtopics.summary: Students learn the definitions and implications of India being a Sovereign, Socialist, Secular, Democratic, and Republic state, including concepts like self-decision making, wealth equality, religious freedom, and elected government.

subtopics.name: 15.3.3. Equal justice to all - Social, Economical and Political Justice
subtopics.summary: Students learn about the concept of equal justice for all citizens in social, economic, and political aspects, including equal opportunities and non-discrimination.

subtopics.name: 15.3.4. Equality
subtopics.summary: Students learn about the principle of equality, meaning everyone should be treated equally irrespective of race, caste, language, religion, etc., and the importance of mutual respect.

subtopics.name: 15.3.5. Freedom of Thoughts, Expression, Faith, Practice and Worship
subtopics.summary: Students learn about the various freedoms guaranteed by the constitution, such as freedom of speech, expression, faith, and worship, and the importance of using these freedoms responsibly.

subtopics.name: 15.3.6. Developing Self Respect, National Integration and Universal Brotherhood
subtopics.summary: Students learn about the importance of self-respect, respecting others, and fostering national integration and universal brotherhood through unity in diversity.

subtopics.name: Fundamental duties
subtopics.summary: Students learn about the ten fundamental duties of Indian citizens, including respecting the constitution, cherishing noble ideals, upholding sovereignty, promoting harmony, preserving heritage, protecting the environment, safeguarding public property, developing scientific temper, and striving for excellence.

subtopics.name: Discrimination
subtopics.summary: Students learn about the concept of discrimination, recognizing that some people are denied equal rights based on factors like religion or educational opportunities, and the importance of ensuring everyone enjoys all rights.

subtopics.name: Four Main Child Rights
subtopics.summary: Lists the fundamental rights: Right to Survival, Right to Protection, Right to Development, and Right to Participation.

subtopics.name: What should be done at home?
subtopics.summary: Guidelines for ensuring child rights are respected within the family environment.

subtopics.name: What should be done at the school?
subtopics.summary: Guidelines for ensuring child rights are respected within the school environment.

subtopics.name: What should be done in the society?
subtopics.summary: Guidelines for ensuring child rights are respected within the community and society.

subtopics.name: Parliament of children
subtopics.summary: Describes the structure and function of child parliaments in advocating for child rights.

subtopics.name: Who cooperates for the protection of child rights?
subtopics.summary: Details the role of the Centre for the Protection of Child Rights and other bodies in safeguarding children's rights.

subtopics.name: 16.6.2. Child Rights Protection Club
subtopics.summary: Details about the Child Rights Protection Club, its purpose, activities, and how children should behave to protect their rights and the rights of others.

subtopics.name: 16.6.3. Special help centre for children - Child Line
subtopics.summary: Information about the Child Line (1098) as a centre for child labourers, street children, and victims of various abuses, explaining its function and how it helps children, including protection from sexual abuse and exploitation (POCSO Act-2012).

subtopics.name: Keywords
subtopics.summary: A list of important vocabulary terms introduced in the chapter related to child rights, including child rights, right to development, child parliament, right to survival, right to participation, Centre for Protection of Child Rights, right to protection, healthy atmosphere, and Child Rights Protection Club.

subtopics.name: 1. Conceptual Understanding
subtopics.summary: Questions designed to assess students' understanding of fundamental child rights concepts.

subtopics.name: 2. Questioning and Hypotheses
subtopics.summary: An exercise prompting students to formulate questions about child rights.

subtopics.name: 3. Experiments - Field Observations
subtopics.summary: An activity encouraging students to observe and identify child rights issues in their local community.

subtopics.name: 4. Information Skills, Projects
subtopics.summary: An activity involving data collection and analysis related to children's well-being and rights within their class.

subtopics.name: 5. Communication through Mapping Skills, Drawing Pictures and Making Models
subtopics.summary: An art-based activity to express children's happiness and rights visually.

subtopics.name: 6. Appreciation, Values and Creating Awareness towards Bio-diversity
subtopics.summary: Questions and activities promoting awareness and action regarding child rights, including addressing child labor and joining protection clubs.

subtopics.name: I can do these
subtopics.summary: A self-assessment checklist for students to evaluate their understanding and ability to act on child rights.

subtopics.name: Suggestions to the Teachers
subtopics.summary: Guidelines for teachers on lesson planning, activity implementation, assessment, and fostering conceptual understanding in Environmental Studies, emphasizing active learning and student participation.

subtopics.name: Academic Standards that have to be achieved by the children through this text book
subtopics.summary: A detailed list of academic standards covering conceptual understanding, questioning, experimentation, information skills, communication, and appreciation towards bio-diversity, to be achieved by students using the textbook.

subtopics.name: Learning Outcomes
subtopics.summary: Specific learning objectives for students in Class 5 Environmental Studies, covering a wide range of topics from basic needs and animal life to body parts, environment, and safety.

subtopics.name: TEXT BOOK DEVELOPMENT TEAM - 2013
subtopics.summary: Lists the editors, contributors, and translators involved in the creation of the textbook.

subtopics.name: QR CODE TEAM
subtopics.summary: Acknowledges the team responsible for integrating QR codes into the textbook, including the QR code ID 1SG1U7.

exercises.text: What do you see in the picture?
Guess... Why was the animal with them?
How are they different from us? Where did they live? What might have they eaten?
Name the different animals that might have been tamed by primitive man. Why did they do so?
Why didn't they tame animals like tiger and lion? Guess...
What animals are tamed by the people now? Why?
How do you feel when you ride on animals?
Talk about the people who use animals for their livelihood.
Observe the diagram given below. Match the name of the animals with the work they are used for.
How much money is left with Lingaiah after working hard for the whole year?
Can he meet his basic needs with the amount left? How?
How many hours does Lingaiah work in a day?
Is Lingaiah getting the benefits he deserve, after so much of hard work or not? Why?
How are the sheep helpful to agriculture?
Is Lingaiah taking good care of his sheep? Write your opinion.
Do you know any shepherds? How is their lifestyle?
Why did the shepherds leave their districts?
Describe the life style of the migrated shepherds.
What did Venkataiah and Kishan say about their difficulties?
Who helped them and how?
Visit an agricultural field. Observe the crops and the creatures living there. Learn how those creatures are useful to the plants.
Discuss with the farmer and list out the names of the creatures which are helping him.
Have you seen the products shown in the pictures? Which animals are these products obtained from?
How are these products used for various decorative purposes?
You have understood that there are many uses of animals. But, are we useful to animals? How?

[VISUAL ACTIVITY] The air we breathe in enters the two spongy red blood vessels called lungs in our chest cavity. The lungs are made up of about 20,000 tiny alveoli. The alveoli are tiny sacs that help in the exchange of oxygen and carbon dioxide. On the walls of the alveoli, there are tiny air sacs called alveoli. The alveoli are also the sites where the body releases carbon dioxide and water vapor. The body's tissues are filled with water and other fluids. The body's tissues are also filled with blood vessels. The blood vessels are made up of red blood vessels and white blood vessels. The red blood vessels carry red blood, while the white blood vessels carry white blood.

When and where does the excretory system work?
Urinary tract
If the food we eat is digested and absorbed, we will produce waste products. The waste products are collected in the urinary bladder.
The excretory system is located on the right side of the vertebral column. It is connected to the urinary bladder by the ureters. The excretory system is also connected to the small intestine by the small intestine.
A pair of kidneys are situated on either side of the vertebral column. The kidneys are responsible for filtering the body's waste products. These impurities are sent out in the form of urine. The urine is then collected in the bladder. The bladder is located on the right side of the vertebral column.

[VISUAL ACTIVITY] 2.4 Layers of the Atmosphere
The air around us is called atmosphere. An consists of many gases, that are present in the air. The atmosphere is divided into two layers: the upper layer and the lower layer.
The upper layer is called the Troposphere. It is the layer where we find the air we breathe. There are 11 Troposphere. The lower layer is called the Stratosphere. It is the layer where we find the air we find in the clouds.
The layers intersect each other. The upper layer is the Troposphere. The lower layer is the Stratosphere. The Troposphere is the layer where we find the air we find in the clouds. All layers of the atmosphere are present in the sky.

[VISUAL ACTIVITY] Kinds of heart failure
Let us now learn about the different types of heart failure.
There is a possibility of heart failure due to a sudden change in the blood pressure. There is a possibility of heart failure due to a chronic illness like asthma, chronic heart disease, or any other heart disease.
In a case of heart failure, the person may feel that their hand is straight while pressing. They may also feel that their chest is tight and they may need to give and receive artificial respiration. They may also need to take the records and talk to the other health workers.

[VISUAL ACTIVITY] 15
OUR CONSTITUTION
15.1 Our Constitution
We have learnt in the previous lesson that, at the end of the 19th century, India was under British colonial rule. The British colonial regime was a period of oppression, exploitation, and neglect of the people. All these people, especially the Indians, were under the control of the British government.
The British government used the Constitution of India to control the people. The Constitution of India was written in the Constitution of India Act of 1947. The Constitution of India Act of 1947 was the first Constitution of India. It was written to protect the rights of the people and to give them a sense of identity and pride.

Mango, tamarind, banyan and citrus trees grow very big. Can we grow them in pots? The art of growing such big, tall trees in pots is called "Bonsai" It is a traditional art of Japan.

The National Green Core was established with the objectives of filling the school surroundings with greenery and protecting the environment. Enjoying the greenery with the work experience, recognizing the hard work, identifying the environmental pollution, explaining about it, creating awareness towards the conservation of environment in the villagers etc. are its other goals. At present it is functioning in high schools of our state with various activities.

Many people migrate from the villages to towns and cities in search of livelihood (jobs). 67% of the population lives in towns. As the population is increasing in cities, the quantum of waste products and problems is also rising. Temperature is increasing along with pollution. So, plans are being made to convert the bio related waste material into manure and use it as a fertilizer to grow vegetables. Crores of rupees is being spent to dispose the garbage in Hyderabad city alone. This problem can be solved by converting the organic garbage into organic manure. Department of Horticulture has planned and implemented the process of making compost from kitchen waste to use it as a manure for plants in pots on the terrace and balcony. Moreover, bags, polythene covers, baskets, tubs, boxes, plastic plates, cement bags, ladders and old tyres are used in growing vegetables.

In the olden days, there used to be an open place (backyard) in the every house. They used to grow the necessary vegetables and leafy vegetables. As the land rates are increasing considerably, houses are constructed in smaller area. So, it is not possible to cultivate vegetables. Every body is depending on the market for procuring vegetables. Chemicals are used to store them for transportation to cities. Such vegetables are not tasty and cause diseases (show their effect on our health) too. We can cultivate healthy vegetables on the terrace of our house. We can grow vegetables that are good for our health in our house itself without using chemical fertilisers and insecticides. Instead we can use organic manure and neem oil to grow the vegetables.
Do you know? The U.N.O. has declared Neem Tree as the Tree of the Century. Neem is the medicine for thousands of diseases.

Our body and face, swell, if the nutrients required for the growth of the body are not taken. Children can not remain active in studies and also while playing games. They look tired. Neither we can study nor play well.

Precautions to be taken to protect our eyes: • Always read in sufficient light (neither very bright nor dim). See that while reading, the distance between the book and your eye is about 30 centimetres. (Measure the distance 30 cm. with a scale). • Do not read while travelling in a bus or train and other vehicles. • We must wash our eyes daily with cold water. • Always watch the television with sufficient light in the room. We must not watch T.V. for too long and from very near. Care must be taken that the distance between the television and our eye must be not less than two and half metres (2.5 m). • If you are not able to see the letters on the black board, you must inform your teachers. • Do not rub your eyes. • Do not use the hand kerchief and towels used by the persons suffering with eye infections like conjunctivities etc. • Consult an eye specialist / doctor if irritation, redness, watering and burning sensation of eyes persists or while reading if you are not able to see the letters clearly and properly.
The doctor who treats the disorders and diseases of the eyes is known as ophthalmologist or eye specialist.

Precautions to be taken to protect our ears: • Close your ears when you hear loud and harsh sounds. • Take proper care to prevent water from entering the ears. Clean your ears with a clean cotton cloth. • Do not use hairpins or match sticks to clean the ears. • If there is pain in your ears or any other problem you must consult a doctor immediately. • Do not put castor oil or any other oil in the ears.
The doctor who treats the problems of ears, nose and throat is called an ENT doctor or ENT specialist

Do you know A bat is a nocturnal mammal (active during the night). It detects objects on its way by its own sounds. It identifies objects/obstacles on its way, when sound waves produced by it reflect to it. It changes its path based on the echo it receives from the objects/obstacles around it.

[IMAGE] 6 OUR BODY - ITS INTERNAL ORGAN SYSTEM
We have learnt about the external organs of the body in the earlier lessons. When we talk about the internal organs of the body, we are referring to the organs that are located inside the body. Let us review the different internal organs and learn about their functions.

1. Which part of the body moves when we breathe?lungs

3. Which part of the body moves when we eat?stomach

[IMAGE] Out of different generations that ruled the Maganpatty states from 1530 A.D. to 1745 A.D. these water resources were used for agriculture. The kings of these states worked hard for the development of agriculture. They constructed 7 large tanks to store water. These tanks were used for growing crops. The kings of these states provided irrigation facilities to farmers to increase the yield of land. Drinking water needs were also served by the tanks.
Do you know?
After India became self-independent, Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel managed all the estates. He also built many roads and bridges. He also built a railway line to connect the villages of the rural areas to the market in the nearest cities.

13.4 Solar Energy
What is solar energy?
Solar energy is the energy that we get from the sun. We use solar energy to generate electricity. We use solar energy to heat our homes. We use solar energy to make food. We use solar energy to produce oxygen.

Do you know? Russia, Canada, China, United States of America, Brazil, Australia are Larger in area when compared to India.
Do you know? As we go upwards from the sea level, for an ascent of every 1000 mtrs. temperature reduces by 6° centigrade.

Do you know? The bottom of the oceans sinks at some places. Due to this, deep pits are formed. These pits are the deepest parts in the oceans. Some of these are as deep as 10,000 mtrs. Some pits are so deep that the Himalayas also will submerge in them. High mountains are also present in the oceans. The mountain ranges are extended in the waters of the Pacific, Indian and Atlantic oceans. The total length of these mountain ranges is about 65,000 kms, and they form the longest mountain ranges on the earth. The crests of some of these mountains extend above the water level of the seas and form Islands.

Do you know? The leaders we elect make laws. These laws are made in the Parliament. The Parliament has two houses namely, The Rajya Sabha and The Lok Sabha. People elect 543 members to the Lok Sabha by casting votes for them. Two members are nominated without elections. 233 members are elected to the Rajya Sabha. 12 members are nominated by the President. The total number of members in the Parliament is 790. Our state has a Legislative Assembly and a Legislative Council. 119 members are elected to Legislative Assembly. They are called the 'Members of Legislative Assembly' (M.L.A.). 40 members are elected to the Legislative Council. They are called 'Member of Legislative Council' (M.L.C.). In our country, the people who are above 18 years of age have the right to vote. He / she can cast his / her vote as per his wish for a candidate contesting in the elections.
